articles.title: 'Heat strokes are part of the job': In Delhi, construction workers endure 43°C heat - Le Monde.fr
articles.full_text:
Even as the thermometer read 43°C on Monday, May 18, Massum Gairulla, a construction worker, carried on as best he could at the site in India's capital. Despite the oppressive heat, about 30 workers toiled away at this upscale neighborhood building in southern Delhi. Here, a frail young man sweated profusely as he shakily stacked a pile of bricks balanced on his head. There, a mason coated a wall with cement. "My whole body aches. I have to take a break every thirty minutes just to get through the day," explained Gairulla, who is barely 21 years old. To cope, he drank four to six liters of water every day.
Headaches, feverishness and vomiting are common for these laborers, who come from India's poorest states and earn just a few hundred rupees (a few euros) per day. After suffering heatstroke the day before, Rachni Devi, a woman in her fifties, was forced to lie down all day in her makeshift shelter cobbled together at the construction site. "I have a fever, body aches, and headaches. I think it's because I was in direct sunlight yesterday," she said, draped in her sari, the end of which she used to cover her head. "Heat strokes are part of the job, so when it happens, we go to the pharmacy, take a pill, and sleep until we can work again," she continued, amid the noise of grinders and hammers.
